On Linux, it's better to (intentionally) not link against libpython. The symbols will be resolved when the extension library is loaded into a Python binary. This is preferable because you might have several different installations of a given Python version (e.g. the system-provided Python, and one that ships with a piece of commercial software). In this way, the plugin will work with both versions, instead of possibly importing a second Python library that will lead to a segfault.

So it's not a big deal, but I think it'd be nice if we could handle indentation a little more gracefully. Previously, the only files that were included by the template were definitions of shell script functions or contents of files to be written to disk (or the version number). This is the first time that the template system is being used to include blocks of code that should be indented relative to the template. As a side effect of the way it currently works and the way the code was initially written, we have slightly off indentation like this and the need to indent the majority of entire files like we see in the `phase-?/main.sh` files.

I'm open to any ideas you have to solve this, but the current approach I have in mind is to stick with the previous approach of when including shell script code, only include function definitions. The way this would work is the current places in `letsencrypt-auto.template` that these files are included become a call to the function defined in that file rather than something to be expanded by `build.py`. Then the `main.sh` files would become standalone functions that could be included by the template anywhere before they are used.

Hopefully this makes sense and let me know if you have any better ideas or questions about what I'm proposing here!

Suggestions to remove it.

```python
# remove mutation
base_response = generate_response(request)
...
if good:
    response = cytoolz.assoc(base_response, 'result', ...)
else:
    response = cytoolz.assoc(base_response, 'error', ...)

# or moving logic into `generate_response`
if good:
    response = generate_response(request, result=...)
else:
    response = generate_response(request, error=...)  
```

Second option should include validation that only one of error/result are provided or a separate response generation functions for error/result.

There shouldn't be a bad effect. Because the topic list permutations were removed, `construct_event_topics` only needs to return a nested list when there are multiple options for a single argument filter.

There was a bug in how the topic list was being generated.  This is an example of a correctly formated topic list to match logs with either of two values for the first indexed event arg:

```
[ event_sig_topic, [option1_for_arg1, option2_for_arg1], arg2_topic ]
```

whereas this is what we were generating to be equivalent to the above; we would pass full topic list permutation against each option:
```
[
    [ event_sig_topic, option1_for_arg1, arg2_topic ],
    [ event_sig_topic, option1_for_arg1, arg2_topic ]
]
```
This topic topic list actual means: any of `[event_sig_topic, option1_for_arg1, arg2_topic ]` in the first position and any of `[event_sig_topic, option1_for_arg1, arg2_topic]` in the second position.  Node filters don't understand nested lists as full topic lists.  The spec defines a single topic list that can have nested lists of options for each positional element.

I ran into a very stange bug when running core tests. After investigating, I found more deep issues:

[This import](https://github.com/openfisca/country-template/blob/master/openfisca_country_template/variables/taxes.py#L12) worked as expected by pure luck. It is not actually a relative import, but an absolute one. Relative imports are not allowed in our context, as the variables files are loaded one by one independently. It turns out the `housing.py` file was parsed first. Then the python importer recorded that it has loaded a module named `housing`. When it parses `taxes.py`, it sees `from housing import ...`. It then used the recorded module. If `taxes.py` was read before `housing.py`, the import would fail 😱.

To avoid this issue, the first thing I tried was to replace `from housing import HousingOccupancyStatus
` by `from openfisca_country_template.variables.housing import HousingOccupancyStatus`. With this the import works in all case, but another issue appears.

In the fast indexed enums, we rely on a [comparison of enums](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-core/blob/enums-perfs/openfisca_core/indexed_enums.py#L75) when comparing an `EnumArray` with an enum item. This only works if the two compared enums are _exactly_ the same object.

With the absolute import, we ended up importing `housing` twice: once when we parse `housing.py`, and another time when we parse `taxes.py` and run into the absolute import. Usually Python deals well with imports, but this doesn't work as we manually import modules from files. The double import make the comparison mentioned in the last paragraph to fail : the two enums compared have been imported separatly, they are not the same object.

Solutions i thought about:

- Make the enum comparison more robust. I didn't find an easy way to do it, and there is a risk to degrade the performances, and we know this part of the code must be fast.
- Fixing the way we import the variable files to avoid double imports. If we record the module`housing.py` as `openfisca_country_template.variables.housing` instead of just `housing`, the double import could be avoided. But I don't see any clean and robust way of doing it.
- Moving all the enums definitions to `base.py`. But it seems pretty heavy and not so  natural. I've reverted it.
- The new solution I implemented, which is avoiding any import by accessing the enum through the `EnumArray`. 

The last one seemed less heavy and more natural to me, but a different opinion on this is very much welcome !

1. If we save identifiers we save them for all transfers, irrespective of them being direct or mediated or else this change will make no sense.

On Python 3, `text_type` is `str` and `str` + `str` works out as expected.
```pycon
>>> u'abc' + str(123)
'abc123'
```

On Python 2, `text_type` is `unicode`. `unicode` + `str` does unicode-promotion on the `str` argument; the `str` of an int is only going to have ASCII characters and so will always be promotable to unicode.
```pycon
>>> u'abc' + str(123)
u'abc123'
```

FWIW, `zope.container.contained.NameChooser` (from which this class derives) also makes the exact same assumptions in its `chooseName` method, freely combining `unicode` and `str`:
```python
        # convert to unicode and remove characters that checkName does not allow
        if isinstance(name, bytes):
            name = name.decode()
        if not isinstance(name, unicode):
            try:
                name = unicode(name)
            except:
                name = u''
        name = name.replace('/', '-').lstrip('+@')

        ...
        # for an existing name, append a number.
        # We should keep client's os.path.extsep (not ours), we assume it's '.'
        dot = name.rfind('.')
        if dot >= 0:
            suffix = name[dot:]
            name = name[:dot]
        else:
            suffix = ''

        n = name + suffix
        i = 1
        while n in container:
            i += 1
            n = name + u'-' + str(i) + suffix
```

I tend to think of components similar to native HTML elements - they have reasonable default CSS built-in, but can be overridden by the parent.

For example, an `<h1>` by default might have

```stylus
display: block
margin-bottom: 16px
```

and an `<a>` by default might have

```stylus
display: inline
margin: 0
```

However either of those can be overridden.

For this specific case it's true we could just make the default `position: absolute`, but it feels weird to me. Maybe for floating toolbars and pop-ups, but not something that looks like a standard block...

Currently, the UI waits until a server response to handle either success or failure. This leaves open the possibility that the UI might feel unresponsive on a slow connection, or even produce an error if the user hits the toggle switch twice. (You can demo this by turning on network throttling in chrome tools.)

Simple solution: disable the toggle switch while the response is pending

More complex: optimistically update vuex and the UI with the intended new value right on-click. A success becomes a no-op, and an error would probably roll back to the previous value.
